subject: Physiology
stem: Which receptor is responsible for the burning pain sensation produced by both capsaicin and temperatures above 43 degrees Celsius?
explanation: TRPV1 (Transient Receptor Potential Vanilloid 1) is activated by capsaicin (the active compound in chili peppers) and by noxious heat above 43 degrees C. Because both stimuli activate the same receptor, they produce the same burning pain sensation. TRPM8 is the cold/menthol receptor. TRPA1 detects noxious cold and pungent chemicals. TRPV4 senses innocuous warmth (~27-35 C) and is mechanosensitive.
highYield: TRPV1 is the capsaicin and noxious heat (>43 C) receptor - both stimuli converge on the same ion channel.

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient applies a mentholated gel to their skin and feels a cooling sensation at room temperature. Which receptor mediates this effect?
explanation: TRPM8 is the cold and menthol receptor, activated at temperatures below 25 degrees C. Menthol activates TRPM8 at room temperature, mimicking the sensation of cold. This is the basis of cooling gels and menthol in toothpaste. TRPV1 responds to heat and capsaicin. TRPA1 detects noxious cold below 10-17 C and pungent chemicals. Piezo2 is a mechanosensory channel for touch and proprioception.
highYield: TRPM8 is activated by menthol and temperatures below 25 C - the molecular basis of menthol cooling sensation.

subject: Physiology
stem: Which TRP channel is activated by mustard oil, allicin from garlic, and temperatures below 10-17 degrees Celsius?
explanation: TRPA1 (Transient Receptor Potential Ankyrin 1) detects noxious cold below 10-17 C and is activated by mustard oil (allyl isothiocyanate), horseradish, garlic (allicin), and cinnamon. All these pungent/irritant chemicals share TRPA1 as their receptor. TRPV1 detects heat and capsaicin. TRPV4 detects innocuous warmth. TRPM8 is the menthol and cool temperature receptor (8-28 C).
highYield: TRPA1 is activated by noxious cold AND pungent chemicals: mustard oil, garlic (allicin), horseradish, cinnamon.

subject: Physiology
stem: Arranging TRP channels from highest to lowest temperature threshold of activation, which of the following sequences is correct?
explanation: TRPV1 activates at >43 C (noxious heat), TRPV4 at ~27-35 C (innocuous warmth), TRPM8 at <25 C (cool to cold), and TRPA1 at <10-17 C (noxious cold). So the order from highest to lowest activation temperature is TRPV1 > TRPV4 > TRPM8 > TRPA1. Option B is wrong because TRPV4 activates at a lower temperature than TRPV1.
highYield: Temperature map: TRPV1 (>43 C hot) > TRPV4 (~30 C warm) > TRPM8 (<25 C cool) > TRPA1 (<17 C noxious cold).

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient with tissue injury develops lowered pain threshold to heat stimuli (thermal hyperalgesia). Which molecular mechanism best explains this phenomenon at the peripheral nociceptor level?
options: Downregulation of TRPM8 receptors by prostaglandins
Sensitization of TRPV1 by prostaglandins and bradykinin via PKC/PKA phosphorylation
Upregulation of Piezo2 channels in Merkel cells
TRPA1 activation by acidic pH at the injury site
explanation: Thermal hyperalgesia (lowered pain threshold to heat after inflammation) is caused by sensitization of TRPV1 by prostaglandins and bradykinin. These inflammatory mediators activate PKC and PKA signaling, which phosphorylate TRPV1 and lower its activation threshold below 43 C, making previously non-painful warm stimuli painful. This is the molecular basis of thermal allodynia. TRPM8 is a cold receptor. Piezo2 mediates touch and proprioception. TRPA1 is activated by cold and pungent chemicals, not pH specifically (though TRPV1 responds to acidic pH).
highYield: Thermal hyperalgesia = TRPV1 sensitization by prostaglandins/bradykinin via PKC/PKA phosphorylation, lowering activation threshold.

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient with myocardial ischemia experiences burning chest pain. This pain is partly attributed to local acidosis at the ischemic tissue. Which receptor directly mediates acid-induced burning pain?
explanation: TRPV1 is activated not only by capsaicin and heat but also by acidic pH (low pH). At sites of tissue injury and ischemia, local acidosis activates TRPV1 on nociceptors, producing the characteristic burning pain. This explains why acidosis in ischemic tissue contributes to the burning quality of ischemic pain. TRPA1 is activated by noxious cold and pungent chemicals. TRPM8 is the cold receptor. Piezo1 is a mechanosensitive channel in vascular endothelium.
highYield: TRPV1 is activated by heat, capsaicin, AND acidic pH - explains burning pain from tissue acidosis in ischemia.

subject: Physiology
stem: Which ion channels are described as the largest ion channels known (>2500 amino acids) and were discovered using a robotic poking assay on cells?
explanation: Piezo1 and Piezo2 are large mechanically activated cation channels, the largest ion channels known with over 2,500 amino acids. They were discovered by Ardem Patapoutian (2021 Nobel laureate) using a robotic poking assay that mechanically stimulated cells to identify which genes were required for mechanosensitivity. TRP channels are much smaller. BK and SK channels are potassium channels involved in repolarization.
highYield: Piezo1 and Piezo2 are the largest ion channels (>2500 amino acids) discovered by Patapoutian using robotic mechanical stimulation.

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient presents with profound ataxia and difficulty with position sense but completely intact pain and temperature sensation. Loss of function mutations in which channel would explain this clinical picture?
explanation: Piezo2 is the principal mechanotransducer in somatosensory neurons and is essential for proprioception (position sense) and light touch sensation. Loss-of-function Piezo2 mutations in humans cause absent proprioception and impaired touch but intact pain - this was confirmed in a landmark NEJM 2016 paper by Chesler et al. The ataxia results from loss of proprioceptive input needed for coordinated movement. Pain is intact because nociception does not depend on Piezo2. TRPV1 mediates heat and pain. Piezo1 is expressed in vascular endothelium and red blood cells, not primarily in somatosensory neurons.
highYield: Piezo2 loss = absent proprioception + impaired touch + ataxia, but intact pain - the key clinical differentiator.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 28-year-old man presents with mild hemolytic anemia, dehydrated red blood cells on peripheral smear (stomatocytes), and a family history of similar findings. Gain-of-function mutations in which mechanosensitive ion channel are responsible for this condition?
explanation: Hereditary xerocytosis (dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis) is caused by gain-of-function mutations in Piezo1. Piezo1 is widely expressed in non-neural tissues including red blood cells. Gain-of-function mutations cause constitutive channel opening, leading to excess cation efflux, cellular dehydration, and the characteristic stomatocyte morphology with hemolytic anemia. Piezo2 is expressed primarily in sensory neurons and its loss causes absent proprioception. TRPV1 and TRPA1 are nociceptive channels unrelated to red blood cell physiology.
highYield: Gain-of-function Piezo1 mutations cause hereditary xerocytosis (dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis).

subject: Physiology
stem: Piezo1 channels expressed in vascular endothelial cells primarily contribute to which physiological function?
options: Vascular tone regulation and blood pressure
explanation: Piezo1 is widely expressed in non-neural tissues including vascular endothelium, red blood cells, and the bladder. In vascular endothelium, Piezo1 senses mechanical forces from blood flow (shear stress) and mediates vascular tone regulation and blood pressure. Nociception is mediated by TRPV1 and other TRP channels. Proprioception depends on Piezo2 in somatosensory neurons. Thermosensation involves TRP channels.
highYield: Piezo1 in vascular endothelium mediates flow-sensing and vascular tone/blood pressure regulation.

subject: Physiology
stem: Piezo2 channels in the skin are expressed in which specialized slow-adapting mechanoreceptors that mediate discriminative touch?
explanation: Piezo2 is expressed in Merkel cells (slow-adapting type 1 mechanoreceptors) in the skin and in dorsal root ganglion neurons. Merkel cells in the skin mediate sustained, discriminative touch. Loss of Piezo2 causes loss of discriminative touch sensation and ataxia from absent proprioception. Pacinian corpuscles respond to vibration and pressure. Meissner corpuscles detect light touch and are rapidly adapting. Ruffini endings respond to skin stretch.
highYield: Piezo2 is expressed in Merkel cells (slow-adapting skin mechanoreceptors) and DRG neurons - mediates discriminative touch.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A patient with postherpetic neuralgia is treated with a high-concentration capsaicin patch (8%, Qutenza). After an initial burning sensation, the patient experiences prolonged pain relief. The mechanism responsible for this sustained analgesic effect is best described as:
options: Blockade of sodium channels preventing action potential generation
Initial TRPV1 excitation followed by receptor desensitization and nociceptor defunctionalization
Upregulation of endogenous opioid release from spinal cord
Selective destruction of A-delta fibers with preservation of C-fibers
explanation: High-dose capsaicin (8% patch) works by first exciting and then defunctionalizing TRPV1-expressing nociceptors. The initial application causes brief burning pain (activation phase) followed by prolonged desensitization - the nociceptors become unresponsive to subsequent stimuli. This process, termed defunctionalization, involves calcium overload, mitochondrial dysfunction, and temporary retraction of peripheral nerve terminals. This mechanism is used for postherpetic neuralgia treatment. It does not block sodium channels directly or upregulate opioids.
highYield: High-dose capsaicin analgesia works by TRPV1 activation followed by defunctionalization of nociceptors, not receptor blockade.

subject: Physiology
stem: The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2021 was awarded to David Julius and Ardem Patapoutian for their discovery of receptors for temperature and touch. Julius used which compound as a molecular probe to identify the heat receptor TRPV1?
explanation: David Julius used capsaicin, the active compound in chili peppers, as a molecular probe to identify TRPV1. By expressing a cDNA library in cells that don't normally respond to capsaicin and screening for capsaicin-responsive cells, Julius identified the capsaicin receptor, later found to also be the heat receptor (activated at >43 C). Menthol was used to identify TRPM8. Mustard oil (allyl isothiocyanate) activates TRPA1. Lidocaine is a sodium channel blocker used as a local anesthetic.
highYield: David Julius used capsaicin as a molecular probe to discover TRPV1, the heat and pain receptor - Nobel 2021.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: Which nucleoside base modification in the mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccines (Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna) prevents recognition by toll-like receptors TLR7 and TLR8, thereby reducing immunogenicity?
explanation: Both BNT162b2 (Pfizer-BioNTech) and mRNA-1273 (Moderna) use complete substitution of every uridine with N1-methylpseudouridine (m1Psi). This modification prevents recognition by TLR7 and TLR8 (endosomal single-stranded RNA sensors) and reduces type I interferon release and cytokine storm. m1Psi also increases translational efficiency and mRNA stability. m6A is a naturally occurring modification but is not the key modification used in COVID vaccines. m5C and N7-methylguanosine are also naturally occurring but not the primary modification used.
highYield: COVID mRNA vaccines use N1-methylpseudouridine (m1Psi) - complete uridine replacement to evade TLR7/TLR8 recognition.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: Pseudouridine (Psi) differs from uridine in a key structural feature that allows it to evade innate immune detection. What is this structural difference?
options: The glycosidic bond is a C-N bond at N3 instead of N1
The glycosidic bond is a C-C bond linking C1' of ribose to C5 of uracil, rather than N1-C1' in uridine
The uracil ring is methylated at C5 position
The ribose sugar is replaced by deoxyribose
explanation: In uridine, the glycosidic bond links C1' of ribose to N1 of the uracil base (N-glycosidic bond). In pseudouridine, the bond is a C-C bond linking C1' of ribose to C5 of uracil - uracil is thus attached through its carbon rather than nitrogen, making it an isomer of uridine. In N1-methylpseudouridine (used in COVID vaccines), there is an additional methyl group at the N1 position of pseudouridine. This structural change alters how TLR7/TLR8 recognize the molecule, preventing immune activation while preserving ribosomal translation fidelity.
highYield: Pseudouridine has a C-C glycosidic bond (C1'-C5 of uracil) unlike uridine's N-glycosidic bond (C1'-N1) - this isomeric difference prevents TLR recognition.

subject: Microbiology
stem: In their landmark 2005 paper published in Immunity, Karikó and Weissman demonstrated that in vitro-transcribed (IVT) mRNA activates which specific toll-like receptors to trigger innate immune responses?
explanation: Karikó and Weissman's 2005 Immunity paper showed that unmodified IVT mRNA is recognized by TLR3 (double-stranded RNA sensor), TLR7 (single-stranded RNA sensor in mice), and TLR8 (single-stranded RNA sensor in humans) as foreign/pathogen-associated RNA. Pseudouridine substitution suppressed recognition by all three. TLR4 recognizes LPS (Gram-negative bacterial cell wall). TLR5 recognizes flagellin. TLR9 recognizes unmethylated CpG DNA. TLR2 and TLR6 recognize bacterial lipoproteins.
highYield: Unmodified IVT mRNA activates TLR3, TLR7, TLR8 - the 2005 Karikó/Weissman Nobel-winning discovery.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2023 was awarded to Katalin Karikó and Drew Weissman for a discovery that enabled effective mRNA vaccines. The prize was awarded specifically for discoveries concerning:
options: Lipid nanoparticle delivery systems for mRNA
Nucleoside base modifications that reduced mRNA immunogenicity
The spike protein structure of SARS-CoV-2
Development of the polymerase chain reaction for mRNA synthesis
explanation: The 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to Karikó and Weissman specifically 'for their discoveries concerning nucleoside base modifications that enabled the development of effective mRNA vaccines against COVID-19.' The key discovery was that replacing uridine with pseudouridine (and later N1-methylpseudouridine) dramatically reduced TLR-mediated innate immune activation, allowing the mRNA to be translated into protein without triggering a destructive immune response. Lipid nanoparticle delivery was a separate (though complementary) development. The spike protein structure was worked out by other researchers (Barney Graham, Jason McLellan). PCR was discovered by Kary Mullis (1993 Nobel in Chemistry).
highYield: Nobel 2023 = Karikó and Weissman, for nucleoside base modifications enabling mRNA vaccines - specifically pseudouridine substitution.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Both BNT162b2 (Pfizer-BioNTech) and mRNA-1273 (Moderna) encode the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein in its prefusion-stabilized conformation. Which specific mutations maintain the spike in prefusion conformation?
options: D614G and E484K mutations in the receptor binding domain
K986P and V987P (two proline substitutions in the S2 domain)
N501Y and K417N mutations in the S1 domain
Furin cleavage site mutations at positions 682-685
explanation: Both COVID-19 mRNA vaccines encode the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein with K986P and V987P - two proline substitutions in the S2 domain that lock the protein in its prefusion conformation. This prefusion-stabilized form exposes the neutralizing epitopes most effectively. These proline substitutions were originally developed by Barney Graham and Jason McLellan for other coronavirus vaccine research. D614G and E484K are naturally occurring variants (not vaccine-engineered). N501Y and K417N are Alpha/Beta variant mutations. Furin site mutations affect cell-to-cell transmission.
highYield: COVID mRNA vaccines encode spike with K986P/V987P proline mutations to stabilize prefusion conformation for optimal immune response.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: In the original Karikó and Weissman 2005 experiments, dendritic cells exposed to pseudouridine-modified mRNA showed which change compared to those exposed to unmodified mRNA?
options: Increased expression of CD80, CD86, IL-6, and IL-12
Significantly reduced upregulation of co-stimulatory markers and pro-inflammatory cytokines
Enhanced antigen presentation via MHC class I pathway
Greater phagocytic activity and increased nitric oxide production
explanation: A key finding of the 2005 Karikó-Weissman paper was that dendritic cells exposed to unmodified IVT mRNA showed potent upregulation of co-stimulatory markers (CD80, CD86) and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-12, TNF-alpha). In contrast, dendritic cells exposed to pseudouridine-substituted mRNA showed significantly reduced activation. This demonstrated that the nucleoside modification effectively silenced the innate immune 'danger signal' inherent in synthetic mRNA, allowing the vaccine to be used without triggering excessive inflammation that would degrade the mRNA before translation.
highYield: Pseudouridine-modified mRNA reduces DC activation (lower CD80, CD86, IL-6, IL-12) compared to unmodified mRNA - key 2005 Karikó/Weissman finding.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: In canonical microRNA biogenesis, the primary miRNA (pri-miRNA) transcript in the nucleus is first cleaved by which enzyme complex?
explanation: In canonical miRNA biogenesis, RNA Pol II transcribes the pri-miRNA. In the nucleus, the Microprocessor complex - consisting of Drosha (an RNase III enzyme) and its partner DGCR8 - cleaves the pri-miRNA to produce a ~70-nucleotide precursor miRNA (pre-miRNA) hairpin. The pre-miRNA is then exported to the cytoplasm by Exportin-5. In the cytoplasm, Dicer (another RNase III enzyme) cleaves the loop to yield a ~21-22 nucleotide duplex, which is loaded into Argonaute 2 to form RISC. Drosha acts first in the nucleus, not Dicer.
highYield: miRNA biogenesis first step in nucleus: pri-miRNA cleaved by Drosha/DGCR8 (Microprocessor complex) to pre-miRNA.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: After export of pre-miRNA to the cytoplasm via Exportin-5, which enzyme cleaves the loop to generate the mature ~21-22 nucleotide miRNA duplex?
explanation: After Exportin-5 exports the pre-miRNA hairpin from nucleus to cytoplasm in a Ran-GTP dependent manner, Dicer (a cytoplasmic RNase III enzyme) cleaves the loop of the hairpin to produce a ~21-22 nucleotide miRNA duplex. One strand (the guide/mature miRNA strand) is then loaded into Argonaute 2 (Ago2) to form the RNA-Induced Silencing Complex (RISC). The passenger strand (miRNA*) is degraded. Drosha acts earlier in the nucleus. RNase H degrades RNA in RNA:DNA hybrids. Ago2 is the final effector but is not the enzyme that cleaves the pre-miRNA loop.
highYield: Dicer (cytoplasmic RNase III) cleaves pre-miRNA loop to generate mature ~22nt duplex - step after Exportin-5 nuclear export.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: In the RNA-Induced Silencing Complex (RISC), which protein contains the catalytic endonuclease activity for cleaving target mRNA, and is the only Argonaute protein with this 'slicer' activity in humans?
explanation: Argonaute 2 (Ago2) is loaded with the mature miRNA guide strand and forms the functional RISC. Ago2 is the only Argonaute protein in humans with endonuclease (slicer) activity - it cleaves target mRNA when there is near-perfect complementarity between the miRNA and the mRNA. With imperfect complementarity (more common in mammals), Ago2/RISC causes translational repression and mRNA destabilization without direct cleavage. Ago1, Ago3, and Ago4 lack slicer activity. Dicer processes the pre-miRNA but does not form RISC. DGCR8 is part of the nuclear Microprocessor complex.
highYield: Ago2 is the only Argonaute with slicer (endonuclease) activity in RISC - cleaves mRNA with near-perfect miRNA complementarity.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2024 was awarded to Victor Ambros and Gary Ruvkun for the discovery of microRNA. What was the model organism used, and what was the first microRNA ever discovered?
explanation: Both Nobel laureates Victor Ambros and Gary Ruvkun worked on Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans, roundworm) as a model organism. lin-4 was the first microRNA ever discovered in 1993 by Ambros. lin-4 regulates larval developmental timing by repressing the lin-14 gene. let-7 was the second miRNA discovered and is significant because it is evolutionarily conserved across species including humans. miR-21 and miR-155 are human cancer-related miRNAs discovered much later. Drosophila and yeast were not the discovery organisms.
highYield: Nobel 2024: first miRNA = lin-4, discovered in C. elegans in 1993 by Ambros; let-7 (second miRNA) is conserved in humans.

subject: Pathology
stem: miR-21 is the most overexpressed microRNA across multiple human cancers. It functions as an oncomiR (oncogenic miRNA) by targeting which set of tumor suppressor genes?
explanation: miR-21 is the prototypical oncomiR and is overexpressed in breast, colon, lung, pancreatic, and ovarian cancers among others. Its oncogenic function is achieved by suppressing tumor suppressor genes including PTEN (phosphatase and tensin homolog - a major negative regulator of PI3K/Akt pathway), PDCD4 (programmed cell death 4 - a translation suppressor), and TPM1 (tropomyosin 1 - cytoskeletal tumor suppressor). RAS and HMGA2 are targets of let-7 (a tumor suppressive miRNA). p53, Rb, BRCA1 are classic tumor suppressors but not the primary miR-21 targets.
highYield: miR-21 (oncomiR) targets PTEN, PDCD4, TPM1 - overexpressed in most human cancers.

subject: Pathology
stem: miR-34a is a tumor suppressor microRNA that is transcriptionally activated by which key cell cycle checkpoint protein, and was the basis for the first miRNA therapeutic to enter clinical trials?
explanation: miR-34a is a direct transcriptional target of p53 (TP53). When p53 is activated by DNA damage or oncogenic stress, it drives transcription of miR-34a, which then suppresses genes promoting cell cycle progression and survival. This creates a tumor suppressive axis. miR-34a is downregulated in many cancers, and its loss promotes cancer progression. MRX34 (liposomal formulation of miR-34a) was the first miRNA therapeutic to enter clinical trials, for liver cancer and other solid tumors. Rb, BRCA1, and CDK4/cyclin D do not directly transcribe miR-34a.
highYield: miR-34a is a p53 transcriptional target (tumor suppressor miRNA); MRX34 was the first miRNA therapeutic in clinical trials.

subject: Microbiology
stem: miR-122 is a liver-specific microRNA with a paradoxical relationship with Hepatitis C virus (HCV). Which of the following best describes this relationship?
options: miR-122 cleaves HCV RNA directly, preventing HCV replication
miR-122 is required for HCV replication; anti-miR-122 (Miravirsen) was tested as an HCV therapeutic
HCV upregulates miR-122 to suppress innate immune responses in hepatocytes
miR-122 promotes HCV entry into hepatocytes by targeting interferon receptors
explanation: miR-122 is paradoxically required for HCV replication - it binds to the 5' UTR of HCV RNA and stabilizes/enhances its replication. This is an unusual case where a host miRNA promotes viral (not host gene) expression. This dependency was exploited therapeutically: Miravirsen (anti-miR-122 locked nucleic acid) was developed as an HCV antiviral - by sequestering miR-122, it reduces HCV replication. miR-122 is also a biomarker for liver injury (elevated in NASH/NAFLD). It does not directly cleave HCV RNA (that would require RISC-mediated cleavage by a viral sequence).
highYield: miR-122 is required for HCV replication; anti-miR-122 (Miravirsen) = novel HCV therapeutic approach.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: miR-451, a red blood cell-specific microRNA important in sickle cell disease and malaria resistance, is unique in its biogenesis because it bypasses which enzyme?
explanation: miR-451 undergoes Dicer-independent biogenesis - it is an exception to the canonical pathway. After Drosha cleaves the pri-miRNA to generate the pre-miRNA (standard first step), the short pre-miR-451 hairpin is loaded directly into Ago2, which performs the cleavage step (bypassing Dicer entirely). This creates a shorter, non-standard mature miRNA. This Dicer-independent pathway is important to remember as an MCQ exception. The red blood cell-specific expression of miR-451 links it to sickle cell disease and malaria (Plasmodium falciparum resistance). Drosha, Exportin-5, and Ago2 are all still used.
highYield: miR-451 (RBC-specific) bypasses Dicer - loaded directly into Ago2 after Drosha cleavage; exception to canonical miRNA biogenesis.

subject: Physiology
stem: let-7 microRNA is downregulated in lung cancer and functions as a tumor suppressor. Which oncogenes are primary targets of let-7-mediated suppression?
explanation: let-7 is an evolutionarily conserved tumor suppressor miRNA that targets the oncogenes RAS (including KRAS, NRAS, HRAS) and HMGA2 (High Mobility Group AT-hook 2, a chromatin remodeling protein). Low let-7 expression in lung cancer correlates with high RAS expression and poor prognosis. This was among the earliest demonstrations that miRNAs regulate oncogenes. PTEN and PDCD4 are targets of miR-21. p53 and Rb are classic tumor suppressors (not oncogenes). VEGF and HIF-1alpha are targets of other miRNAs.
highYield: let-7 suppresses RAS and HMGA2 oncogenes; downregulated in lung cancer with poor prognosis.

subject: Medicine
stem: A genomic epidemiology study finds that a specific haplotype on chromosome 3p21.3 is the major genetic risk factor for severe COVID-19, carried by approximately 50% of South Asians and 16% of Europeans. The origin of this risk haplotype is traced to introgression from:
explanation: A landmark genomic study identified that a haplotype on chromosome 3p21.3 inherited from Neanderthals is the major genetic risk factor for severe COVID-19 (requiring ICU care and mechanical ventilation). This region carries several genes involved in immune and inflammatory responses. The haplotype is carried by approximately 50% of South Asians and 16% of Europeans (populations with significant Neanderthal ancestry). Interestingly, a separate Neanderthal-inherited haplotype provides protection against severe COVID-19. This was a key example of paleogenomics having direct clinical relevance. Denisovans introgressed mainly into East Asian/Melanesian populations.
highYield: Neanderthal-introgressed haplotype on chr 3p21.3 = major genetic risk for severe COVID-19; carried by ~50% South Asians.

subject: Medicine
stem: The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2022 was awarded to Svante Paabo 'for his discoveries concerning the genomes of extinct hominins and human evolution.' A previously unknown hominin lineage called Denisovans was identified from a finger bone found in which location?
explanation: Denisovans were identified from a 40,000-year-old finger bone fragment found in Denisova Cave in the Altai Mountains of Siberia. The DNA sequence did not match Neanderthal or modern human sequences, leading to the recognition of a previously unknown hominin lineage. The cave is named after an 18th-century hermit Denis. The Neander valley in Germany is where Neanderthal fossils were first found (hence 'Neanderthal'). Olduvai Gorge is a key Homo habilis/Homo erectus site. Flores Island is where Homo floresiensis ('hobbit') was found.
highYield: Denisovans discovered from finger bone in Denisova Cave, Altai Mountains, Siberia - Nobel 2022 (Svante Paabo).

subject: Biochemistry
stem: Ancient DNA from Neanderthal specimens is highly degraded and shows a characteristic chemical damage pattern used to authenticate its antiquity. What is this hallmark chemical damage pattern?
options: G-to-A transitions at the 5' ends of DNA fragments
C-to-T transitions (cytosine deamination) particularly at fragment ends
A-to-G transitions throughout the sequence uniformly
Depurination causing A and G removal creating abasic sites throughout
explanation: Ancient DNA is authenticated in part by a characteristic damage pattern: cytosine deamination, which causes C-to-T transitions (cytosine converts to uracil, which is read as thymine during sequencing). This damage is most pronounced at the ends of DNA fragments and creates a distinctive 'damage signature' that distinguishes genuine ancient DNA from modern contamination (modern contaminant DNA does not show this end-damage pattern). Paabo's lab developed methods to detect and computationally correct for this damage pattern. The best-preserved ancient DNA comes from permafrost or cool cave environments that slow chemical degradation.
highYield: Ancient DNA authentication: C-to-T transitions (cytosine deamination) concentrated at fragment ends - hallmark damage pattern.

subject: Physiology
stem: Tibetans show remarkable adaptation to high-altitude hypoxia partly due to a gene variant introgressed from Denisovans. Which gene confers this advantage and what protein does it encode?
options: HIF-1alpha (hypoxia inducible factor 1-alpha) introgressed from Neanderthals
EPAS1 encoding HIF-2alpha (part of hypoxia-inducible pathway), introgressed from Denisovans
VHL (von Hippel-Lindau) tumor suppressor from Denisovans promoting erythropoiesis
VEGF from Neanderthals promoting high-altitude angiogenesis
explanation: Tibetans carry a Denisovan-introgressed variant of the EPAS1 gene, which encodes HIF-2alpha (Hypoxia Inducible Factor 2-alpha), a key component of the oxygen-sensing/hypoxia-inducible pathway. This Denisovan EPAS1 variant was positively selected in Tibetans because it confers survival advantage at high altitude by blunting the typical erythropoietic response to hypoxia (which in non-adapted populations causes excessive polycythemia and coagulation problems). This is the classic textbook example of adaptive introgression from archaic hominins into modern humans. VHL regulates HIF degradation. VEGF is a downstream HIF target.
highYield: Tibetan altitude adaptation = Denisovan-introgressed EPAS1 variant (encodes HIF-2alpha) - classic adaptive introgression example.

subject: Medicine
topic: Paleogenomics - Neanderthal DNA in Modern Humans
stem: Modern humans of non-African ancestry carry approximately 1-4% Neanderthal DNA due to interbreeding that occurred approximately 70,000 years ago. Which population carries the highest percentage of Denisovan DNA, and what percentage?
options: East Asians, carrying up to 8% Denisovan DNA
Melanesians/Papua New Guineans, carrying up to ~5% Denisovan DNA
South Asians, carrying up to 10% Denisovan DNA
Europeans, carrying up to 6% Denisovan DNA
explanation: When Homo sapiens migrated out of Africa, they interbred with Neanderthals in western Eurasia and with Denisovans in eastern Eurasia and Southeast Asia. Melanesians and Papua New Guineans carry the highest percentage of Denisovan DNA - up to approximately 5%. This reflects the geographic range of Denisovans extending through Southeast Asia and Oceania. Non-African modern humans carry 1-4% Neanderthal DNA. Africans have minimal Neanderthal or Denisovan introgression (having not encountered these groups during the out-of-Africa migration). East Asians have some Denisovan ancestry but lower than Melanesians.
highYield: Melanesians/Papua New Guineans carry up to ~5% Denisovan DNA - highest of any modern human population.

subject: Physiology
stem: TRPV4 is distinct from other TRPV family members in that it is activated by innocuous warmth and serves a dual role. What additional non-thermal function does TRPV4 perform?
options: Nociception for noxious heat above 43 degrees C
Acid pH detection in tissue injury
explanation: TRPV4 is activated in the innocuous warm range (~27-35 C), making it distinct from TRPV1 (which requires noxious heat >43 C). In addition to thermosensation, TRPV4 is also mechanosensitive and is involved in osmosensing. It responds to hypotonicity (cell swelling) and mechanical stimuli. This dual role in temperature and mechanical/osmotic sensing reflects the broader polymodal nature of TRP channels. TRPV1 handles noxious heat. TRPA1 handles noxious cold. Acid pH is sensed by TRPV1.
highYield: TRPV4 senses innocuous warmth (~27-35 C) AND is mechanosensitive/osmosensitive - dual thermal and mechanical roles.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: In mRNA COVID-19 vaccines, the m1Psi-modified mRNA is encapsulated in lipid nanoparticles (LNPs). What is the primary function of the LNP delivery system?
options: To enhance TLR7/TLR8 recognition for adjuvant effect
To protect mRNA from extracellular RNase degradation and facilitate cellular uptake via endosomal fusion
To directly encode the spike protein along with the mRNA
To activate T-regulatory cells and prevent vaccine-induced autoimmunity
explanation: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) serve as the delivery vehicle for mRNA vaccines. Their primary functions are: (1) protecting the fragile mRNA from extracellular RNase enzymes that would rapidly degrade naked mRNA, (2) facilitating cellular uptake through endocytosis, and (3) enabling endosomal escape via membrane fusion, releasing the mRNA into the cytoplasm where ribosomes can translate it. This is complementary to (but separate from) the pseudouridine modification that reduces immunogenicity. LNPs are not immunological adjuvants that enhance TLR recognition - that would defeat the purpose of the m1Psi modification. LNPs do not encode proteins.
highYield: LNPs protect mRNA from RNase degradation and enable endosomal escape for cytoplasmic delivery - complementary to m1Psi modification.

subject: Physiology
stem: An image shows a schematic of a nociceptor nerve terminal with multiple ion channels labeled. Channel X is activated by temperatures above 43 degrees C, capsaicin, and acidic pH. It is a non-selective cation channel with preference for Ca2+. Channel X also shows sensitization by prostaglandins leading to thermal hyperalgesia. Based on the Nobel Prize 2021, which channel is X?
explanation: The description matches TRPV1 perfectly: activated by noxious heat (>43 C), capsaicin (the hot chili pepper compound used by Julius to discover it), and acidic pH. It is a non-selective cation channel with significant calcium permeability. Sensitization by prostaglandins and bradykinin via PKC/PKA phosphorylation underlies thermal hyperalgesia - the molecular basis of allodynia in inflammation. TRPV1 was discovered by David Julius using capsaicin as a molecular probe (Nobel 2021). TRPA1 is for noxious cold and pungent chemicals. TRPM8 is the cold/menthol receptor. Piezo2 is a mechanosensory channel for proprioception.
highYield: TRPV1: heat (>43 C) + capsaicin + acid pH + Ca2+ permeant + sensitized by prostaglandins = thermal hyperalgesia.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 58-year-old man presents 6 hours after acute chest pain. Troponin I levels are mildly elevated. A blood miRNA panel shows markedly elevated miR-208 and miR-1. These miRNA biomarkers are elevated because they are released from which tissue during ischemic injury?
options: Skeletal and cardiac muscle cells undergoing necrosis
Endothelial cells of the coronary arteries
explanation: miR-208 is heart-specific (cardiac myosin heavy chain-associated) and miR-1 is highly expressed in skeletal and cardiac muscle. Both are released into the circulation when myocardial cells undergo necrosis during myocardial infarction. They have been studied as potential biomarkers for acute MI, similar to but potentially more sensitive than troponin in early hours. miR-1 elevation is also seen in muscular dystrophies. miR-122 is the liver-specific miRNA (elevated in liver injury including NASH). Endothelial cells release different miRNA profiles. Platelets release miR-21 and miR-223 during activation.
highYield: miR-208 (heart-specific) and miR-1 (cardiac/skeletal muscle) are circulating biomarkers for myocardial infarction.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A researcher studies a miRNA that binds to the 3'-UTR of a target mRNA with imperfect (partial) complementarity in mammalian cells. What is the expected primary mechanism of gene silencing in this scenario?
options: mRNA cleavage by Ago2 endonuclease at the complementary site
Translational repression and mRNA destabilization without direct cleavage
Nuclear export block preventing mRNA from reaching ribosomes
explanation: When a miRNA binds its target mRNA with imperfect/partial complementarity (which is most common for mammalian miRNAs), the primary mechanism of silencing is translational repression combined with mRNA deadenylation and destabilization - NOT direct cleavage. The RISC complex containing Ago2 recruits deadenylases and decapping enzymes that lead to mRNA decay, while simultaneously blocking ribosome elongation. Near-perfect complementarity (more common in plants) triggers direct Ago2-mediated cleavage. Nuclear export block and promoter methylation are not direct miRNA silencing mechanisms.
highYield: Imperfect miRNA:mRNA complementarity (mammalian) causes translational repression + mRNA destabilization, NOT cleavage.
